topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the stark finality of a closing door. Professor Albright was leaving. My stomach clenched, a tight, cold knot forming just beneath my ribs. I reread the subject line three times, a slow, deliberate process that felt more like an interrogation than reading. I remember the warmth of his office, the way he’d leaned back, his eyes alight with intellectual fervor when discussing my proposal. Now, that warmth felt like a sudden, icy withdrawal. I stood up abruptly, the cheap plastic chair scraping loudly against the linoleum floor. My hands were shaking slightly as I picked up my laptop, the screen reflecting a stark, unblinking light. "This can't be happening," I muttered, the sound thin and brittle in the quiet library annex. I started pacing the small space, my footsteps echoing hollowly, a restless energy thrumming through my limbs.

[story 2]

When the announcement finally filtered down from the department meeting, I felt a distinct tightening in my chest, like a vise slowly squeezing the air out of my lungs. I watched the faces around me—some nodding politely, others exchanging knowing glances—and I felt utterly isolated in my sudden surge of agitation. I gripped my coffee mug so tightly my knuckles turned white, the ceramic digging painfully into my palms. I didn't look up when the news was confirmed; instead, I focused intently on the condensation tracing slow paths down the side of the mug. Later that evening, I found myself re-reading the last chapter he’d annotated, tracing the ink with my finger, a strange, hot flush rising up my neck. "He just... walks away?" I asked my roommate, my voice sounding sharper than I intended, laced with an edge of disbelief that bordered on sharp resentment.

[story 3]

The news hit me during my lunch break, mid-bite of a sandwich. The crunch of the bread seemed amplified, jarring against the sudden, heavy stillness that descended over my senses. I set the sandwich down with unnecessary force, the movement jerky. My jaw tightened until I could feel the strain in my molars. For a long moment, I just stared at the patterned tile floor, cataloging the grout lines as if they held the secret to the universe's sudden betrayal. A slow burn started in my throat, a hot, prickly sensation that demanded release. I stood up, needing to move, needing to articulate the sheer unfairness of it all. I walked out into the courtyard, breathing in the cool, indifferent air, my shoulders rigid, every muscle coiled tight with a profound sense of being overlooked and dismissed.
